office:text.text:p:
7. giåhur peš10 a-šà? nu-x saÑ [ma-an-si-ga]1
Var.: giåapin a-åà x x saÑ ma-an-si-ga.

text:p.text:span: saying (the words) of Amar-Sin, your servant:
In the matter of the field that my liege spoke to me about:
Because I am my king’s servant,
as soon as I saw his majesty’s face, I dropped my weapons (to deal with the issue)
I (?) dispatched (someone) to the field in the territory.
In the plans, the riverbank did not … the field; it’s side seems much too short for me
That field does not (even) come close to the river by forty (fifty) km.
The irrigation ditch does not wind around the Hursag.
no deceit here; (I am obedient) to the orders that my liege has given.
That field cannot be lost (to cultivation)!
Whatever you, my king order me to do, (I will do) … Now my king knows (all this)!

office:text.text:p: Two texts of unknown origin form the basis for the reconstruction, and both are unreliable. X1 is idiosyncratic, difficult to read, and is broken on the right edge. X2 is known only from a hand copy made years ago by Th. Pinches that was found by Irving Finkel, who was kind enough to make it available to me. The original has not been located. Until the Relph text can be found, or new manuscripts surface, this provisional edition will have to suffice.

text:p.text:span: . These lines are preserved only in the old copy by Th. Pinches and it is not at all clear that he drew the signs correctly.
, “wet place.” The admittedly speculative interpretation of
is based on K. Maekawa’s discussion of the use of
. This line, with a discussion of the verb
, “to draw near,” had been cited in M. Civil,

refers here to a mountain range/ridge, or valley, so that the line might have to be translated: “the irrigation ditch does not wind around the ridge/valley.” The current rendition is based on the notion that
this is a reference to a specific topographical feature in the vicinity of Apiak, the one that gave us the fortification name
. I have assumed that the first word is the rare
There is a note on the side of the copy that reads: “Relph, no. 16. 59 mm. high, 79 mm. long”

office:text.text:p: Tablet typology: compilation tablet: X1. The rest are all Type III. X2 is in landscape format.

text:p.text:span: oo oo o o o o o o o o
X1 . x x x o o o o o o
N1 + + + . o o o + . o o o
- . o o + + + o o o o
N1 + + . o o o o o o o o o
The reverse of X2 has traces of three lines after line 9, followed by a double line and then:
It is not certain that this is still part of the same text, although
’s of this type usually only have one composition.
